title: Syncable
summary: A protocol for model types that declare how to derive a global identifier for Ensembles deduplication.
rawIndexableTextContent: A protocol for model types that declare how to derive a global identifier for Ensembles deduplication.   Overview Global identifiers let Ensembles recognize when objects created independently on different devices represent the same logical entity, and merge them instead of duplicating. There are two ways to provide a global identifier: Simple: Single Property Override globalIdentifierKey with the name of a stored property:  Advanced: Computed Identifier (Core Data) Override globalIdentifier(from:) for compound or computed identifiers. The parameter is typed as Self, so you get direct property access:  globalIdentifier(from:) only works with NSManagedObject subclasses (Core Data). For SwiftData models, use globalIdentifierKey instead. A global identifier must never change once it has been assigned to an object. Changing an identifier after sync has begun will cause Ensembles to treat the object as a different entity, leading to duplicates or data loss. Use a value that is set once at creation time, such as a UUID string. A UUID is usually the best choice for a global identifier. Use a fixed, meaningful value (like a name) only for singleton objects or reference data like tags, where two devices might independently create the same logical object. When model types conform to Syncable, CoreDataEnsembleContainer and SwiftDataEnsembleContainer discover the identifiers automatically — no delegate or closure needed.

summary: Sync SwiftData models with Ensembles using SwiftDataEnsembleContainer.
rawIndexableTextContent: Sync SwiftData models with Ensembles using SwiftDataEnsembleContainer. Overview The EnsemblesSwiftData module provides SwiftData-native APIs for Ensembles sync. You never need to touch Core Data directly — just pass your @Model types and get a syncing ModelContainer back. Complete Example: SwiftData with CloudKit Here’s a complete, working setup for a SwiftUI app that syncs SwiftData models via CloudKit. 1. Define Your Models  2. Set Up the Container  That’s the complete setup. The container creates a ModelContainer, attaches to the cloud, and syncs automatically. Your @Query properties update automatically when remote changes arrive, thanks to persistent history tracking (enabled by default). 3. Use in Views  No extra save calls needed — SwiftData auto-saves, which triggers Ensembles to sync. Using a Custom Store Location If you need control over where the SQLite file lives:  Global Identifiers with `Syncable` When two devices independently create the “same” object (e.g., a tag with the same name), Ensembles needs a global identifier to recognize they represent the same entity and merge them instead of duplicating. SwiftData models declare a global identifier by conforming to the Syncable protocol:  The globalIdentifierKey names a property whose value uniquely identifies the object across all devices. When two objects of the same entity have the same global identifier value, Ensembles merges them into one. A global identifier must never change once it has been assigned to an object. Changing an identifier after sync has begun will cause Ensembles to treat the object as a different entity, leading to duplicates or data loss. A UUID is usually the best choice for a global identifier — assign it once at creation time and it’s guaranteed unique across all devices:  Use a fixed, meaningful value instead when two devices might independently create the same logical object — for example, singleton settings objects or tags that are identified by name:  Not all entities need global identifiers — only those where independent creation of “the same” object is likely (reference data, categories, user profiles). Entities that are always created explicitly by the user (photos, drawings) typically don’t need them. Entities that do not conform to Syncable (and have no global identifier provided via a closure) are synced normally but are not deduplicated. If the same logical object is created independently on two devices, both copies will exist after sync. Auto-Sync Configuration Control when syncing occurs:  Set autoSyncPolicy to .manual and call container.sync() yourself for full control. Responding to Events  Unlike CoreDataEnsembleContainer, the SwiftData container does not need to merge changes into a view context — persistent history tracking (enabled by default) causes @Query properties to update automatically. Use didSaveMergeChanges for additional processing such as refreshing non-SwiftData UI state. Computed Identifiers (Core Data) For compound or computed identifiers on NSManagedObject subclasses, override globalIdentifier(from:) instead of globalIdentifierKey. The parameter is typed as Self, so you get direct property access:  This method is called only when globalIdentifierKey is empty (the default when you don’t override it). globalIdentifier(from:) only works with NSManagedObject subclasses. For SwiftData @Model types, use globalIdentifierKey. Conflict Resolution Ensembles resolves concurrent changes automatically using attribute-level last-writer-wins (see Conflict Resolution). For most apps this works transparently — but because each attribute is resolved independently, a merge can combine values that are individually valid but together violate a business rule (e.g., startDate from one device and endDate from another, where start ends up after end). With Core Data, you can intercept and repair these cases using the reparation context delegate methods. SwiftData models don’t have NSManagedObject subclasses, so those delegate methods aren’t directly useful. The best approach for SwiftData is to design your model so that attribute-level merging always produces valid results: Avoid cross-attribute invariants where possible. For example, store a startDate and a duration instead of startDate and endDate — either attribute can be updated independently without breaking validity. Use computed properties for derived values instead of storing them. A stored total that must equal quantity * price will break if quantity and price are updated on different devices. Compute it instead. Validate after sync by observing didSaveMergeChanges and checking invariants in your app logic. Fix invalid combinations by saving corrected values — those corrections propagate as new events. If you need the full reparation context for complex conflict handling, access the underlying CoreDataEnsemble via .coreDataEnsemble and set its delegate directly. Note that this replaces the internal delegate bridge, so you’ll need to handle global identifiers and save notifications yourself:  Using SwiftDataEnsemble Directly If you manage your own ModelContainer or need more control, use SwiftDataEnsemble directly:  Access the underlying CoreDataEnsemble via the .coreDataEnsemble property for advanced operations:  Model Versioning with VersionedSchema When you first ship your app, pass your current @Model types to modelTypes: — this is all you need for a single model version. When your model evolves (e.g., you add a property to Note), you need to tell Ensembles about all versions so it can accept sync events recorded against any of them. Do this by switching from modelTypes: to migrationPlan:. 1. Define Versioned Schemas Move your model types into VersionedSchema enums — one per version:  2. Create a Migration Plan  3. Use `migrationPlan:` Instead of `modelTypes:`  Ensembles converts each VersionedSchema into a Core Data model and passes all of them to the underlying CoreDataEnsemble. This allows version compatibility checking during sync — events from a device still running V1 can be correctly merged on a device running V2, and vice versa. Persistent History Tracking SwiftDataEnsembleContainer and SwiftDataEnsemble enable persistent history tracking by default. This allows SwiftData to detect changes that Ensembles merges into the store, so your @Query properties stay up to date automatically. If you provide custom persistentStoreOptions, make sure to include these keys:

rawIndexableTextContent: The central class of Ensembles, representing a set of synchronizing persistent stores.   Overview An ensemble monitors a single Core Data persistent store, records local saves as events, and exchanges these events with peers via a CloudFileSystem backend to reach eventual consistency. You typically create one CoreDataEnsemble for each persistent store you need to sync. Ensemble instances with the same ensembleIdentifier on different devices sync their persistent stores together. Lifecycle Create the ensemble with your store URL, model URL, and a cloud backend. Call attachPersistentStore(seedPolicy:) to register with the cloud and import existing store data. Avoid saving to the persistent store during attachment. Call sync(options:) periodically to exchange data with peers. Call detachPersistentStore() to stop syncing and remove local sync data. All async operations are serialized internally — only one attach, detach, or sync runs at a time.

Understand how Ensembles resolves conflicts and how to customize the process.
rawIndexableTextContent: Understand how Ensembles resolves conflicts and how to customize the process. Overview When multiple devices change the same data concurrently, conflicts arise. Ensembles resolves these automatically using causal revision tracking, and provides delegate hooks for cases that need custom logic. Automatic Resolution Every event carries a revision set that records which events from other peers have been seen. When events are replayed during a merge, the integrator determines the causal ordering: if one change “happened after” another (it had seen the other’s revision), that change wins. For truly concurrent changes to the same attribute, the event with the highest globalCount (a monotonically increasing counter across the device’s event history) is used as a tiebreaker. Conflict resolution operates at the attribute level: each attribute is resolved independently using last-writer-wins (LWW). For to-many relationships, Ensembles uses an add-wins set strategy — additions from both sides are preserved, and only explicit deletions are removed. Ensembles provides eventual consistency: all devices that have seen the same set of events will converge to the same state, regardless of the order in which events were received. This means that for most apps, conflicts are resolved transparently without any code on your part. The Reparation Context For cases where automatic resolution isn’t sufficient, the delegate provides a two-context pattern for inspecting and repairing merged data before it’s committed. Inspecting Changes Before the merge save, the ensemble calls:  The savingContext contains all the merged changes about to be committed. Use it to inspect what will happen:  Making Repairs Do not modify the savingContext directly. Instead, make corrections in the reparationContext:  Changes made in the reparation context are captured as new events, so they propagate to all peers on the next merge. The saving context and reparation context have a parent-child relationship. Do not nest perform or performAndWait calls between them — this will deadlock. Handling Save Failures If the merge save fails (e.g., due to a validation error), the ensemble calls:  Inspect the error to determine which objects caused the failure, make corrections in the reparationContext, and return true to reattempt the save. Return false to abort. SwiftData The reparation context operates on NSManagedObjectContext and NSManagedObject, so it isn’t directly usable from SwiftData @Model types. The recommended approach for SwiftData is to design your model so that attribute-level merging always produces valid results. See SwiftData Integration for specific strategies. Global Identifiers and Deduplication When the same logical object is created independently on multiple devices, automatic merging produces duplicates — two objects with different identifiers but the same meaning (e.g., two “Work” tags). To handle this, implement the global identifier delegate method:  When Ensembles encounters objects with matching global identifiers from different devices, it merges them into a single object rather than creating duplicates. Global identifiers must be immutable — once assigned, an object’s identifier must never change. If you need to change an identifier, delete the object and create a new one. Forced Detaches In exceptional circumstances, the ensemble may be forced to detach: The cloud identity changed (e.g., the user signed out of iCloud) The hardware identity changed unexpectedly Data corruption was detected (incomplete mandatory events) The device’s registration was removed from the cloud When this happens, the ensemble calls coreDataEnsemble(_:didDetachWithError:). You should update your UI to reflect the detached state and can attempt to re-attach to resume syncing.
title: Conflict Resolution

title: Migrating from Ensembles 2
summary: Transition an existing Ensembles 2 (Objective-C) app to Ensembles 3.
rawIndexableTextContent: Transition an existing Ensembles 2 (Objective-C) app to Ensembles 3. Overview Ensembles 3 is fully backward compatible with Ensembles 2 cloud data. You can migrate without a data reset — existing cloud files, event stores, and CloudKit records are all readable by E3 out of the box. What’s Backward Compatible Cloud files — The directory structure and file formats in the cloud are identical. E3 reads all E2 cloud data. Event store — The Core Data model for the local event database (CDEEventStoreModel) is unchanged. JSON format — Property change values use the same JSON serialization. CloudKit records — Record types (CDEFileSystemNode, CDEItem, CDEDataFile) and field names are preserved. Encryption — E3’s .legacy encryption format (RNCryptor v3) matches E2’s CDEEncryptedCloudFileSystem. Reading E2 data works regardless of compatibility mode — compatibility mode only restricts what E3 exports. Step-by-Step Migration 1. Swap the dependency Replace the Ensembles 2 CocoaPod or framework with the Ensembles 3 Swift package:  Or add it via Xcode’s Add Package Dependencies menu. 2. Set compatibility mode If some users may still be running the E2 version of your app, set CompatibilityMode.ensembles2Compatible so E3 restricts its exports to formats E2 peers can parse:  3. Update API names Ensembles 3 uses different class and method names: Ensembles 2 (Objective-C) Ensembles 3 (Swift) CDEPersistentStoreEnsemble CoreDataEnsemble CDEPersistentStoreEnsembleDelegate CoreDataEnsembleDelegate leechPersistentStore(...) CoreDataEnsemble/attachPersistentStore() deleechPersistentStore(...) detachPersistentStore() mergeWithCompletion: sync(options:) CDECloudFileSystem CloudFileSystem CDEEncryptedCloudFileSystem EncryptedCloudFileSystem CDECloudKitFileSystem CloudKitFileSystem  4. Test thoroughly Verify your app can read existing cloud data from E2 peers. Confirm that E2 peers can still read data exported by your E3 build (while in .ensembles2Compatible mode). Test attachment, sync, and detach cycles. 5. Switch to full E3 mode Once all users have upgraded to the E3 version of your app, change the compatibility mode to unlock future E3-only features:  What `ensembles2Compatible` Restricts Currently, CompatibilityMode.ensembles2Compatible applies one restriction: Model hashes: Forces compressModelHashes to false, so model version hashes use the full XML property list format that E2 peers can parse. No other export behavior is changed. As E3 evolves, additional restrictions may be added to this mode to maintain E2 compatibility.

rawIndexableTextContent: License validation for Ensembles.  Overview Free backends (CloudKit, LocalFile, Memory) and SwiftData work without a license. Any other cloud backend — including custom CloudFileSystem implementations — requires a valid license key. Call activate(_:) once at app launch with a license key obtained from Mental Faculty.  The license key is validated by signature, bundle prefix, and SDK coverage. The key’s expires date must be on or after this SDK version’s release date — this ensures the subscription was active when this version was released. Once an app ships with a covered SDK version, it keeps working forever.

rawIndexableTextContent: Sync Core Data stores across devices using event sourcing. Overview Ensembles synchronizes Core Data persistent stores by recording local saves as events, exchanging them through a pluggable cloud backend, and replaying them on each peer to reach eventual consistency. An CoreDataEnsemble monitors a single persistent store. When the app saves, Ensembles captures the changes automatically. Calling sync(options:) downloads remote events, replays them into the local store, and uploads new local events. Conflicts are resolved through causal revision tracking, with delegate hooks for custom repair logic. Quick Start The simplest way to add sync is with CoreDataEnsembleContainer, which creates the Core Data stack, handles the delegate, and auto-syncs on save:  For more control, use CoreDataEnsemble directly:  Cloud Backends Ensembles ships with several cloud backends: CloudKitFileSystem — Syncs via iCloud using CloudKit. GoogleDriveCloudFileSystem — Syncs via Google Drive REST API v3. OneDriveCloudFileSystem — Syncs via Microsoft OneDrive (Graph API v1.0). PCloudCloudFileSystem — Syncs via pCloud, a Swiss provider with strong privacy guarantees. WebDAVCloudFileSystem — Syncs via any WebDAV server (Nextcloud, ownCloud, etc.). EncryptedCloudFileSystem — Wraps any backend with AES-256-GCM encryption. LocalCloudFileSystem — Uses a local directory, useful for testing or shared-folder sync. MemoryCloudFileSystem — An in-memory actor-based backend for unit testing. Any storage that can hold files at paths can serve as a backend. See Custom Cloud Backends for a guide to implementing your own. Backward Compatibility Ensembles 3 reads and writes Ensembles 2 cloud data and event store formats. Existing apps can migrate without a data reset. See Migrating from Ensembles 2 for a step-by-step guide.
title: Ensembles

summary: Implement the CloudFileSystem protocol to sync via any storage service.
rawIndexableTextContent: Implement the CloudFileSystem protocol to sync via any storage service. Overview Ensembles exchanges sync data through a CloudFileSystem — a protocol that abstracts file storage behind a simple path-based API. Any service that can store and retrieve files at paths can serve as a backend: cloud storage (S3, Google Cloud Storage), file servers (WebDAV, SFTP), databases, or even peer-to-peer connections with a local cache. The CloudFileSystem Protocol The core protocol has 8 requirements organized into four groups: Connection  isConnected — Return whether the backend is ready for operations. connect() — Establish a connection. Called before each attach or merge. fetchUserIdentity() — Return a token identifying the current user, or nil if identity tracking is not supported. Ensembles uses this to detect account changes (e.g., signing out of iCloud). File Operations  Paths are forward-slash separated, rooted at /. The ensemble creates a directory structure like /MyEnsemble/events/, /MyEnsemble/baselines/, etc. Directory listings should return CloudFile or CloudDirectory items with their path and name populated. Only list immediate children — do not recurse. File Transfer  Upload copies a local file to the remote path. Download copies a remote file to the local path. Both paths are absolute on their respective systems (local paths are filesystem paths; remote paths are within the cloud file system’s namespace). A Minimal Example Here is a skeleton implementation to illustrate the structure:  Reference Implementations Study the built-in backends as starting points: MemoryCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesMemory) — The simplest implementation. An actor that stores files in a dictionary. Good for understanding the protocol’s expectations. LocalCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesLocalFile) — Uses FileManager to store files in a local directory. Shows how to map the protocol onto a real file system. CloudKitFileSystem (in EnsemblesCloudKit) — A production backend using CloudKit. Shows how to implement batch operations and setup hooks. PCloudCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesPCloud) — A URLSession-based REST client with no SDK dependency. A good model for building backends against any REST API. BoxCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesBox) — Wraps the Box Swift SDK. Demonstrates how to bridge an ID-based API (where files are identified by numeric IDs, not paths) onto the path-based CloudFileSystem protocol using folder-walking and caching. S3CloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesS3) — Wraps the AWS SDK for Swift. Shows how to simulate directory semantics on a flat key-value store using key prefixes and delimiters. EnsemblesBox and EnsemblesS3 (along with EnsemblesDropbox, EnsemblesZip, and EnsemblesMultipeer) require an external SDK. With the source distribution, enabling the corresponding package trait resolves this automatically. With the binary distribution (XCFrameworks), you must add the external SDK as a separate package dependency — see the Available Backends table in the README for details. Optional: Batch Operations If your backend can handle multiple files in a single request (reducing round trips), adopt CloudFileSystemBatchOperations:  The fileUploadMaximumBatchSize and fileDownloadMaximumBatchSize properties control how many files Ensembles passes per batch call. Default is 10 if you don’t override them. Optional: Setup Hooks For backends that need initialization, caching, or repair logic, adopt CloudFileSystemSetup:  Threading and Sendable Cloud file systems must conform to Sendable. If your implementation uses mutable state, you have several options: Use actor isolation (as MemoryCloudFileSystem does) Use @unchecked Sendable with explicit locking (as LocalCloudFileSystem does) Make the type stateless, delegating state to a thread-safe service All protocol methods are async throws, so you can freely call async APIs in your implementation.

rawIndexableTextContent: Understand how Ensembles synchronizes Core Data stores using event sourcing. Overview Ensembles uses an event-sourcing architecture. Every change to a Core Data persistent store is recorded as an event. Events are exchanged between devices through a pluggable cloud backend and replayed to bring each device’s store into a consistent state. Event Sourcing Model When the app saves to the monitored persistent store, a SaveMonitor captures the inserted, updated, and deleted objects and records them as a modification event in a separate event store database. Each event records the full set of property changes and is tagged with a revision that tracks causal ordering across peers. The event store is a separate Core Data stack from your app’s store — Ensembles never modifies your model or schema. Events are stored locally until they are exported to the cloud, and remote events are imported and kept locally until they are integrated. The Sync Flow A call to sync(options:) proceeds through several phases: 1. Preparation The ensemble connects to the cloud backend, verifies the device’s identity (cloud account and hardware), and checks that the device is still registered. If identity has changed (e.g., the user signed out of iCloud), the ensemble forces a detach. 2. Import New data files, baselines, and events are downloaded from the cloud and stored locally. File downloads can be batched for backends that support CloudFileSystemBatchOperations. 3. Baseline Consolidation Old events are compacted into a baseline snapshot. A baseline captures the full state of the store at a point in time, allowing events before that point to be discarded. This keeps the sync data from growing without bound. 4. Rebasing When the event history grows large, the ensemble can rebase — compressing the history relative to the latest baseline. Rebasing runs automatically when Ensembles estimates a significant space saving, or can be triggered with forceRebase. 5. Integration Remote events are replayed into the persistent store in three stages: Insert — New objects are created in the store. Update — Attributes and relationships are updated. When multiple peers have changed the same attribute, the value from the most recent event (by causal ordering) wins. Delete — Objects marked as deleted are removed. Before the integrated changes are saved, the delegate can inspect and repair them via coreDataEnsemble(_:shouldSaveMergedChangesIn:reparationContext:). 6. Export New local events, baselines, and data files are uploaded to the cloud. This phase can be suppressed with suppressCloudFileDeposition. 7. Cleanup Outdated remote files (events that have been superseded by baselines) are removed from the cloud. Key Components Component Role CoreDataEnsemble Main coordinator. Serializes all operations via an internal AsyncStream. EventStore Manages the local event database (a separate Core Data stack). CloudManager Orchestrates cloud file operations: snapshot, import, export, cleanup. EventIntegrator Replays remote events into the app’s persistent store. SaveMonitor Observes NSManagedObjectContextDidSave to capture local changes. RevisionManager Tracks causal ordering of events across distributed peers. BaselineConsolidator / Rebaser Consolidates event history into compact snapshots.  Cloud Backends All cloud backends implement the CloudFileSystem protocol. This is the primary extension point. See Custom Cloud Backends for a guide to adding your own. Two optional protocols extend the base: CloudFileSystemBatchOperations — Adds batch upload, download, and delete for backends that support them (reduces round trips). CloudFileSystemSetup — Adds one-time preparation, priming, and directory repair hooks. Model Versioning Each event is stamped with a model version hash (either a full entity-hash dictionary or a compressed MD5). During integration, RevisionManager checks every incoming event’s model version against the local managedObjectModels array — a list of all NSManagedObjectModel instances the app supports. When hashes match, the event is integrable and proceeds through the sync flow. When hashes don’t match, the event is flagged with EnsembleError.unknownModelVersion and excluded from integration. This prevents applying changes that reference entities or attributes the current app version doesn’t understand. Providing Model Versions For Core Data projects using .xcdatamodeld bundles, the URL-based convenience initializers on CoreDataEnsemble automatically load all model versions from the bundle via loadAllModelVersions(from:). For SwiftData projects, SwiftDataEnsemble builds in-memory models from @Model types or VersionedSchema entries. You can also pass managedObjectModels directly to the designated initializer when you have in-memory models from other sources. The `managedObjectModels` Array If managedObjectModels is nil, model version checking is disabled and all events are treated as integrable. This is appropriate for testing but not recommended in production. If the array is non-nil, it must contain all model versions that any peer in the sync group might produce. When a peer upgrades its schema and starts emitting events with a new model hash, other peers must also be updated to include that model version or they will reject those events. Handling Breaking Migrations Ensembles does not perform Core Data model migration itself — it only checks compatibility. If you add or rename entities or attributes, you must: Include both the old and new NSManagedObjectModel in the managedObjectModels array. Perform any necessary lightweight or custom migration on the app’s persistent store before attaching the ensemble. Ensure all peers in the sync group are updated to understand the new schema before they sync events from upgraded peers. Data Flow Diagram  Local saves flow right: captured by the event store, then exported to the cloud. Remote changes flow left: imported from the cloud into the event store, then integrated into the app’s store.

title: remove(_:)
rawIndexableTextContent: Removes the given element and all elements subsumed by it.  The element of the set to remove. Return Value The intersection of [member] and the set, if the intersection was nonempty; otherwise, nil. Discussion In the following example, the .priority shipping option is removed from the options option set. Attempting to remove the same shipping option a second time results in nil, because options no longer contains .priority as a member.  In the next example, the .express element is passed to remove(_:). Although .express is not a member of options, .express subsumes the remaining .secondDay element of the option set. Therefore, options is emptied and the intersection between .express and options is returned.

rawIndexableTextContent: Set up Ensembles to sync a Core Data persistent store across devices. Overview Ensembles synchronizes a Core Data SQLite store by recording local changes as events and exchanging them through a cloud backend. This guide walks through everything you need, starting with a complete working example using CoreDataEnsembleContainer. For SwiftData, see SwiftData Integration. Add Ensembles to Your Project Add the Ensembles package to your Package.swift or via Xcode’s package dependencies:  Import the core module and one cloud backend:  Complete Example: Core Data with CloudKit Here’s a complete, working setup for a SwiftUI app that syncs via CloudKit. This uses CoreDataEnsembleContainer, which creates the Core Data stack for you and syncs automatically. 1. Create Your Core Data Model In Xcode, add a .xcdatamodeld file to your project (e.g., Model.xcdatamodeld). Define your entities as usual. For this example, we’ll assume a Note entity with title (String) and uniqueID (String) attributes. 2. Define Your Model with `Syncable` For deduplication, conform your NSManagedObject subclass to the Syncable protocol and specify which property holds the global identifier. A UUID assigned at creation time is usually the best choice:  A global identifier must never change once it has been assigned. Changing it after sync has begun will cause duplicates or data loss. Use a fixed, meaningful value instead of a UUID for singleton objects or reference data like tags, where two devices might independently create the same logical object. 3. Set Up the Container  That’s the complete setup. The container discovers Syncable conformances automatically, attaches to the cloud, and begins syncing. Remote changes are merged into viewContext so your UI stays up to date. 4. Respond to Events (Optional)  Using a Custom Store Location If you need control over where the SQLite file lives, pass an explicit storeURL:  Working with an Existing Core Data Stack If you already have an NSPersistentContainer, pass it in directly:  Or if you have an existing NSManagedObjectContext:  Auto-Sync Configuration Control when syncing occurs with AutoSyncPolicy:  Set autoSyncPolicy to .manual and call sync() yourself for full control over timing. Backward Compatibility with Ensembles 2 If you’re transitioning from Ensembles 2 and some users may still be on E2, set the compatibility mode:  This restricts exports to formats that Ensembles 2 peers can parse. Switch to .ensembles3 (the default) once all users have upgraded. The Manual Way: CoreDataEnsemble For more control over the sync lifecycle, use CoreDataEnsemble directly. This is useful when you need to manage attach/detach timing, handle specific delegate methods, or integrate with a complex Core Data stack. Create and Attach  The ensembleIdentifier must be the same on all devices that should sync together. If you have multiple persistent stores, create one ensemble per store with a unique identifier. Avoid saving to the persistent store while attaching. If a save is detected, attachment will fail with an error and you will need to retry. Sync Changes Call sync(options:) periodically — for example, when the app enters the foreground, on a timer, or in response to a push notification:  A sync downloads remote events, replays them into the local store, and uploads new local events. Sync errors are usually transient — just retry later. Handle the Delegate Implement CoreDataEnsembleDelegate to respond to sync events. The most important method merges remote changes into your main context:  If your NSManagedObject subclasses conform to Syncable, you don’t need to implement globalIdentifiersForManagedObjects — the ensemble discovers identifier properties automatically. You only need the delegate method if you use CoreDataEnsemble directly without CoreDataEnsembleContainer. Other delegate methods let you: Inspect and repair merge data before it’s committed (shouldSaveMergedChangesIn:reparationContext:) Handle save failures during merging (didFailToSaveMergedChangesIn:error:reparationContext:) React to forced detaches caused by identity changes or data corruption (didDetachWithError:) See Conflict Resolution for more on conflict handling and the reparation context. Detach To stop syncing, detach from the cloud:  This removes local sync data (event logs and metadata). The persistent store itself is not affected. You can re-attach later to resume syncing. Seed Policies The SeedPolicy parameter controls how existing data in the store is handled during attachment: SeedPolicy.mergeAllData (default) — Imports local data so it can be merged with data from other devices. SeedPolicy.excludeLocalData — Ignores local data. Use this when you want to start fresh from cloud data. Sync Options For advanced control, pass SyncOptions:  Process Pending Changes On iOS, the app may be suspended before Ensembles finishes recording a save. Call processPendingChanges() — for example, in a background task — to ensure all changes are fully stored:  Monitor Activity Track long-running operations via currentActivity and activityProgress, or observe these notifications: coreDataEnsembleDidBeginActivity — An activity started coreDataEnsembleDidMakeProgressWithActivity — Progress updated (includes SyncingPhase or AttachingPhase) coreDataEnsembleWillEndActivity — An activity finished (may include an error)

rawIndexableTextContent: Attaches the ensemble to corresponding ensembles on other devices.  Controls how existing data in the persistent store is treated. Defaults to SeedPolicy.mergeAllData. Discussion Attachment sets up local storage and metadata, registers the persistent store in the cloud, and converts the contents of the local store into transaction logs for syncing with other devices. If an error occurs, the ensemble remains in a detached state and you must reattempt attachment later. EnsembleError.disallowedStateChange if already attached, or EnsembleError.saveOccurredDuringAttaching if a save to the store was detected during the attach process. Avoid saving to the persistent store while attaching.
